perturbation.original_text: “Business Day ” means any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or statutory holiday in the Province of Quebec
perturbation.changed_text: “Business Day ” means any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or statutory holiday in the City of Montreal
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Province of Quebec' to 'City of Montreal' narrows the scope of what constitutes a business day. This is an in-text contradiction since it implies statutory holidays observed only in Montreal would affect timelines, even if the parties operate province-wide or nationally. This change in 'Section 1. Definitions' by changing the scope from 'Province of Quebec' to 'City of Montreal' creates ambiguity. For example, if Concordia had offices in other cities of the province, only the office in Montreal might consider the City's holidays.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A Party may terminate this Agreement, without cause, upon twenty (20) Business Days’ prior written notice to the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Province of Quebec' to 'City of Montreal' in the definition of 'Business Day' creates a strong in-text contradiction. The contradicted text states that a party can terminate the agreement with 'twenty (20) Business Days’ prior written notice.' If 'Business Day' is now defined only in relation to Montreal, it introduces ambiguity as to which holidays affect the termination notice period, especially if the parties operate province-wide. This is a clear and direct conflict because the original, broader definition ensured consistent application across the Province, while the narrowed definition creates uncertainty and potential disputes over the termination timeline. This is an example of misaligned terminology leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” means any information which is confidential in nature or that is treated as confidential by a Party and that is furnished or transferred by or on behalf of such Party (the “ Disclosing Party ”) to the other Party (the “ Receiving Party ”), whether such information is or has been conveyed verbally or in written or tangible form, and whether such information is acquired d irectly or indirectly such as in the course of discussions, site visits or other investigations by the Receiving Party, including but not limited to, scientific, technical, financial or business information, data, ideas, concepts or know -how, formula, anal ysis, process, design, sketch, photograph, plan, drawing, specification, sample, rep ort, study, finding, prototype and non -published patent application s, that are considered and treated as being confidential by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” means any information which is confidential in nature or that is treated as confidential by a Party and that is furnished or transferred by or on behalf of such Party (the “ Disclosing Party ”) to the other Party (the “ Receiving Party ”), whether such information is or has been conveyed verbally or in written or tangible form, and whether such information is acquired d irectly or indirectly such as in the course of discussions, site visits or other investigations by the Receiving Party, including but not limited to, scientific, technical, financial or business information, data, ideas, concepts or know -how, formula, anal ysis, process, design, sketch, photograph, plan, drawing, specification, sample, rep ort, study, finding, prototype and non -published patent application s, that are considered and treated as being confidential by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Disclosing Party' to 'Receiving Party' in the definition of 'Confidential Information' creates a significant contradiction. It now suggests that information treated as confidential by the *recipient* is considered confidential, regardless of the discloser's intent. This conflicts with the fundamental principle of a non-disclosure agreement, which protects the discloser's information. The change in Section 1 alters the entity that determines the confidentiality of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party agrees that it shall not: 2.1.1 use any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for any reason other than the Purpose; or 2.1.2 disclose, disseminate or otherwise communicate, in whole or in part, any of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Disclosing Party' to 'Receiving Party' in the definition of 'Confidential Information' directly contradicts the Contradicted Text (specifically 2.1.1 and 2.1.2). The original text and contradicted text were aligned, such that the Receiving Party was obligated to not disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information. Now, according to the Changed Text, information is considered 'Confidential Information' if the *Receiving Party* considers it confidential, regardless of the Disclosing Party's intentions. This makes the Receiving Party's obligations regarding the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information ambiguous and unenforceable, as the Receiving Party now defines what is confidential.

perturbation.original_text: “Corporate Advisors ” means any lawyers, accountants, financial and other professional advisors of Receiving Party who are required by law, by contract or otherwise to keep all Confidential Information that may be disclosed to them by Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Corporate Advisors ” means any lawyers, accountants, financial and other professional advisors of Receiving Party who are permitted by law, by contract or otherwise to keep all Confidential Information that may be disclosed to them by Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "required" to "permitted" alters the obligation of Corporate Advisors to maintain confidentiality. The original text enforces that corporate advisors *must* keep confidential information private. However, the change allows them to disclose the confidential information. This creates a contradiction because the Receiving Party may believe their corporate advisors are obligated to protect the information, but that's no longer the case.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving P arty may disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to those of its officers, directors, employees, (including those of its Affiliates) or Corporate Advisors who have a need to know such Confidential Information provided that such individuals are bound by obligations of confidentiality to the Receiving Party or have entered into agreements with the Receiving Party with obligations of confidentiality no less stringent than those of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from "required" to "permitted" in the definition of "Corporate Advisors" directly contradicts the contradicted text. The contradicted text states that Corporate Advisors must be 'bound by obligations of confidentiality.' By changing 'required' to 'permitted', the Corporate Advisors are no longer necessarily bound by confidentiality, thus creating a direct conflict and making the confidentiality obligations unclear and unenforceable, as the Receiving Party may disclose confidential information to Corporate Advisors who are not required to maintain confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: “Retained Information ” shall mean one (1) copy of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information that is retained by the Receiving Party : (i) if and to the extent such Confidential Information is required by law, regulation, administrative or court order , and (ii) where such Confidential Information is stored as electronic data due to automatic archiving and back up procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: “Retained Information ” shall mean one (1) copy of the Receiving Party’s Confidential Information that is retained by the Receiving Party : (i) if and to the extent such Confidential Information is required by law, regulation, administrative or court order , and (ii) where such Confidential Information is stored as electronic data due to automatic archiving and back up procedures.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Disclosing Party's" to "Receiving Party's" within the definition of 'Retained Information' introduces an in-text contradiction. As the receiving party, it cannot retain a copy of it's own confidential information under the 'Retained Information' definition, as it is originally defined as the Disclosing party's information being retained by the Receiving Party. By changing this, it alters the purpose and scope of the NDA, and creates uncertainty about what can be retained and what must be destroyed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Unless otherwise requested in writing by the Disclosing Party within fifteen (15) days of the expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement, or upon written notice given at any time during the Term of this Agreement requesting the return of the Disclosing P arty’s Confidential Information , following the expiration of the fifteen (15) day delay provided for above the Receiving Party shall destroy all tangible forms and destroy/delete all intangible forms of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information (includin g any originals and all copies thereof) in its possession. Notwithstanding the foregoing, t he Receiving Party shall be permitted to retain one copy of the Retained Information and such Retained Information shall be treated by the Receiving Party in accor dance with this Agreement. Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall provide to the Disclosing Party or shall cause to be provided to the Disclosing Party, a written confirmation executed by a duly authorized officer of the Re ceiving Party that all originals and any copies of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information (other than Retained Information) or any portion thereof have been returned or destroyed , as the case may be, and shall no longer be used in any manner whatsoeve r by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Disclosing Party's Confidential Information" to "Receiving Party's Confidential Information" in the definition of 'Retained Information' creates a contradiction. The contradicted text specifies that the Receiving Party must destroy all tangible and intangible forms of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, except for 'Retained Information'. By changing the definition of Retained Information to include the Receiving Party's own confidential information, it becomes unclear what the Receiving Party is obligated to destroy versus retain. The contradicted text assumes 'Retained Information' is a subset of 'Disclosing Party's Confidential Information', which is no longer true after the change, creating a clear and direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date, and unless earlier terminated in accordance herewith, shall continue in force for a period of [NUMBER OF] year(s) from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date, and unless earlier terminated in accordance herewith, shall continue in force for a period of one (1) year(s) from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the term length from '[NUMBER OF] year(s)' to 'one (1) year(s)' generates a contradiction because if the rest of the agreement references '[NUMBER OF]' amount of years, there is now a clear contradiction, as well as ambiguity to the obligations required by both parties. It also creates uncertainty about what information is included within this time frame, as well as liability if this time is extended.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Parties agree that each Party shall be free to 6 disclose the following without prior authoriz ation of the other Party: the names of the Parties; the nature of the relationship established herein; and, the duration of the Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text used '[NUMBER OF] year(s)' allowing for a variable agreement duration. The changed text fixes the duration to 'one (1) year(s)'. The contradicted text refers to 'the duration of the Agreement'. If '[NUMBER OF]' was intended to be a different number of years than one, then the changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text by providing conflicting information about the agreement's duration, creating an in-text contradiction.
